script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for MEME. Friday — here is your MEME recap. The portfolio closed one point five two percent up on the session. A strong finish to the week, led by a handful of meaningful catalyst-driven moves across the book.

AST SpaceMobile surged more than five percent on Friday after B. Riley analyst Mike Crawford upgraded the stock to Buy from Hold, keeping his price target at eighty-five dollars per share. That upgrade landed on a day when the name was already drawing fresh institutional attention — and for a position this size in the portfolio, the timing matters.

No broad macro context is available for Friday's session. Index-level data was not captured for this episode. Listeners should note that the absence of a market-wide catalyst makes the individual name moves inside this portfolio more significant — the gains here were earned on fundamentals and analyst conviction, not a rising tide.

AST SpaceMobile was the clear headline of the session. B. Riley's Mike Crawford flipped his rating to Buy, citing the stock's current valuation as cheap enough to justify the risk. His price target held at eighty-five dollars per share. The upgrade came against a backdrop of ongoing debate — one comparison piece framed the company against SpaceX, noting both are burning cash at scale but with sharply different paths to profitability. A separate piece on Seeking Alpha acknowledged the stock has dropped nearly sixty percent from its highs, arguing dilution was the catalyst but weakening sentiment was the real driver. What Friday's price action tells you is that the market responded to the analyst call with conviction. The stock added five point zero seven percent. For this portfolio, AST SpaceMobile sits at six point two nine percent of total weight — a meaningful position that had a meaningful day.

Turning now to the full holdings rundown, starting with the largest position. Applied Optoelectronics was up two point one six percent on Friday. A piece examining Lumentum's artificial intelligence laser chip demand touched on broader optical networking themes that directly apply to Applied Optoelectronics — record laser chip shipments and expanding capacity across the sector are tailwinds worth monitoring heading into next week.

Redwire Corporation was flat on the session, closing unchanged at zero point zero zero percent. No specific news crossed on Friday. The industrials space saw mixed performance broadly, and Redwire's flatline reflects a quiet tape for the name. The next meaningful catalyst will likely come from contract announcements or earnings guidance.

AST SpaceMobile, as covered in depth, gained five point zero seven percent. The B. Riley upgrade and the broader analyst conversation around valuation made this the most active story in the portfolio on Friday.

Over at Nebius Group, the stock rose three point four six percent after the company announced a seven hundred seventy-five million dollar secured debt facility, priced at SOFR plus two point five zero percent and maturing in two thousand thirty. The deal directly addressed dilution fears that had sent the stock down fourteen percent on Thursday. Microsoft and Meta contracts were cited as backing the expansion. Jim Cramer separately flagged concern about semiconductor and high-flying artificial intelligence names, though the market largely ignored that caution for Nebius on Friday.

Bloom Energy added three point nine eight percent on the session. A profile piece noted the stock has returned nine hundred twelve percent since July twenty twenty-one and is up thirty-five point one percent over the past six months. Separately, a pipeline rejection related to an Oracle data center plan raised questions about project timing and costs — a development worth watching for any data center energy provider.

TeraWulf gained one point zero zero percent. No specific news crossed for the name on Friday. The broader bitcoin mining and artificial intelligence infrastructure theme continues to provide sector-level support, but the one percent move reflects a quiet individual session.

Lumentum Holdings rose three point seven seven percent. A new actively managed exchange-traded fund launched under the photonics theme, carrying a zero point six four percent expense ratio and targeting laser and optical chip companies powering artificial intelligence data centers. Lumentum is a core holding in that thematic space, and fresh institutional product launches around the sector signal continued interest.

Meanwhile, IREN Limited fell three point four seven percent on Friday. No specific news drove the decline. The name sits at five percent of the portfolio, and the move likely reflects profit-taking or sector rotation within the artificial intelligence infrastructure trade after recent strength.

Sandisk Corporation dropped three point nine nine percent on the session, the largest single-name decline in the portfolio on Friday. No specific news was attributed to the move. Storage and memory names have faced pressure intermittently as the market debates the pace of enterprise spending cycles. This is the name to watch most closely for a stabilization signal heading into next week.

Three data points to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, any follow-through analyst commentary on AST SpaceMobile following Friday's B. Riley upgrade — price target revisions from other firms would be significant. Second, Nebius Group's debt facility closing confirmation and any additional contract disclosures tied to the Microsoft and Meta relationships. Third, broader artificial intelligence infrastructure spending signals from any technology sector commentary over the weekend, which could set the tone for IREN and Sandisk on Monday's open.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for MEME. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch for Monday's open on the artificial intelligence infrastructure names — the divergence between winners and laggards that started Friday is unlikely to resolve quietly.
